name: ASCILINE-2.0
tagline: Real-time video rendered as ASCII art and streamed over the network
description: ASCILINE-2.0 converts live video into ASCII or pixel-art text frames and streams them in real time over WebSockets. It's part art experiment, part engineering challenge — turning a webcam feed or video file into animated characters, viewable in a browser or terminal, with zero GPU required.
forWho: Developers and creative coders who appreciate clever engineering. Terminal enthusiasts. Anyone who's ever thought 'what if I could stream a movie as text characters?' Honestly, it's as much for the love of building it as anything else.
story: This started as a one-night experiment. Can I take a webcam feed and render it as ASCII characters fast enough to look like smooth video? The answer was yes — but the real challenge was making the network streaming efficient. Raw ASCII frames are huge. I built a custom adaptive codec (RAW, ZLIB, and DELTA modes) that achieved about 375x wire savings on delta frames. What started as a weekend project became a real engine I'm genuinely proud of.
details: Python backend using FastAPI and OpenCV for frame capture and ASCII conversion. Five color modes from pure monochrome up to full 16-million color. Adaptive codec automatically switches between RAW (first frame), ZLIB (compressed full frame), and DELTA (only changed characters) based on frame content. LAN streaming support for local network multi-viewer setups. JavaScript frontend renders frames in a browser canvas or raw terminal.
detailedSpecs: Frame pipeline: OpenCV VideoCapture reads frames from webcam or file source → resize to target column/row dimensions → convert to grayscale (for luminance mapping) → map each pixel's brightness to a character from a density-ordered ASCII ramp (configurable, default 10-char ramp from space to '@'). Color modes: MONO (single color attr), 8-COLOR (ANSI 8), 256-COLOR (ANSI 256-color cube), TRUECOLOR (24-bit RGB per character), and PIXEL-ART (2x1 block characters for higher fidelity). Codec: frame 0 always sent as RAW JSON array. Subsequent frames compared to previous frame; if diff ratio < 15% → DELTA packet (only changed cell indices + new values, ~375x smaller than raw on static scenes); if diff ratio ≥ 15% → ZLIB-compressed full frame. WebSocket server via FastAPI's websockets support; clients negotiate codec preference on connect. Frontend: JS canvas renderer draws characters cell-by-cell using fillText; terminal renderer outputs ANSI escape sequences directly. Target frame rate: 24fps at 180×50 character resolution on LAN.
